title: podman does not forward ipv6 ports - podman as root
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**
Note: This issue is about running podman as **root**.  It is very similar to #4311, but the cause seems to be different https://github.com/containers/libpod/issues/4311#issuecomment-545243940 

podman does not forward ipv6 requests to the container, only ipv4.
Trying to make nginx available via ipv6 fails. Connecting from the host using [::1]:PORT fails, wheras connecting to the nginx server via [::1]:PORT from inside the container works. Also, connecting from the host using 127.0.0.1:PORT works. Connecting to the container vial ipv6 should work or it should be clearly documented that `--publish` only works for connections via ipv4.
<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. make a fresh fedora container with nginx installed

2. run container via `podman run --name nginx-static -t -a STDOUT -a STDERR -a STDIN --rm -p 8080:80/tcp  localhost/fednginximage bash`

3. inside the container run `/usr/sbin/nginx`

4. from the host, try to connect to the nginx server via both, `[::1]:8080` and `127.0.0.1:8080`

**Describe the results you received:**
Connecting from the host only works for `127.0.0.1:8080`, but fails for `[::1]:8080`

**Describe the results you expected:**
Connecting from the host should work for both cases.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:            1.6.2
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.12.10
OS/Arch:            linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

    - container_id: 1
      host_id: 231072
      size: 65536
  MemFree: 6668619776

  OCIRuntime:
    name: runc
    package: runc-1.0.0-95.rc9.gitc1485a1.fc30.x86_64
    path: /usr/bin/runc
    version: |-
      runc version 1.0.0-rc9+dev
      commit: 0840802d505e28d3adcbc7a275cbeadbdf64ddec

  SwapFree: 8407478272
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```

```

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body: This issue had no activity for 30 days. In the absence of activity or the "do-not-close" label, the issue will be automatically closed within 7 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
I believe that the CNI guys say this is the right behaviour?  Should we close?
I think we need to positively verify if recent Docker does forward v6 ports before we close definitively
Closing, reopen if we find that Docker does this.

> Closing, reopen if we find that Docker does this.

docker-1.13.1-108 on CentOS7 works with IPv6 by default.

docker-compose.yml excerpt:
  ports:
    - "10051:10051"

ss -anp | grep 10051:
tcp    LISTEN     0      128    [::]:10051              [::]:*                   users:(("docker-proxy-cu",pid=4697,fd=4))

That's just a proxy service holding the port open so it can't be used by any applications on the host - is it actually adding rules to forward them? `ip6tables -nvL` and `ip6tables -t nat -nvL` would help.

> is it actually adding rules to forward them? `ip6tables -nvL` and `ip6tables -t nat -nvL` would help.

Nope. 4tables contains only default "CNI firewall plugin rules" for 10.88/16, ip6tables is empty.

I've managed to implement a fix using xinetd binding to specific ipv6 address + "redirect = 127.0.0.1 443".

I believe docker does do IPv6 by default. My test case:

```
$ sudo podman run -d --rm -p 10030:1080 --name=socks5 serjs/go-socks5-proxy
$ curl -s --socks5-hostname [::1]:10030 https://www.google.com | wc 
curl: (7) Failed to connect to ::1 port 10030: Connection refused
      0       0       0
```

Versus:

```
$ sudo docker run -d --rm -p 10030:1080 --name=socks5 serjs/go-socks5-proxy
$ curl -s --socks5-hostname [::1]:10030 https://www.google.com | wc 
     14     352   13894
```

In my case this was the underlying problem with an issue I've been debugging. I have two Ubuntu 20.04 server installs, and switching from docker to podman was failing on one - turned out one had `::1` included for localhost in /etc/hosts, causing downstream failures due to the above.
Comparing the `ss -anp` output between the two, the difference is docker's is bound to `*:10030`, vs podman's `0.0.0.0:10030`.
